questions.question_text: What is the minimum age to obtain a Mississippi real estate license?
questions.explanation: Mississippi requires applicants for a real estate salesperson license to be at least 18 years of age. Applicants must also be Mississippi residents and complete 60 hours of pre-license education.

questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education must a salesperson applicant complete?
questions.explanation: Mississippi requires 60 hours of approved pre-license education for salesperson applicants. This education must be completed before taking the PSI licensing exam.

questions.question_text: Who administers the Mississippi real estate licensing exam?
questions.explanation: Mississippi's real estate licensing exam is administered by PSI Services LLC under contract with MREC. The salesperson exam has two portions: 80 national questions (70% to pass) and 40 state-specific questions (75% to pass).

questions.question_text: The Mississippi salesperson state exam portion contains 40 questions. What passing score is required?
questions.explanation: The Mississippi salesperson licensing exam has two portions: 80 national questions requiring 70% (56 points) to pass, and 40 state-specific questions requiring 75% (30 points) to pass. Both portions must be passed to obtain a license. The broker state portion requires 80%.

questions.question_text: A salesperson candidate passes the national portion at 70% but scores 72.5% on the state portion. What is the result?
questions.explanation: The state portion requires 75% (30 of 40) to pass, so 72.5% (29 of 40) is a failing score. The national portion requires 70%, which she met. Per PSI's rules she retakes only the failed state portion, scoring at least 75%.

questions.question_text: Which type of criminal conviction can disqualify a license applicant?
questions.explanation: Under §73-35-10(2)(b), any felony conviction within 5 years bars licensure. Felonies involving fraud, dishonesty, breach of trust, or money laundering are a permanent bar regardless of when they occurred.

questions.question_text: An applicant has a non-fraud felony conviction from 7 years ago. How does MREC handle this?
questions.explanation: Under §73-35-10(2)(b), felonies within 5 years of application are an automatic bar, and felonies involving fraud, dishonesty, breach of trust, or money laundering are barred at any time. A non-fraud felony older than 5 years is not an automatic bar — MREC evaluates the applicant under the statute's public-trust standard.

questions.question_text: A felony involving fraud or dishonesty occurred 10 years ago. What is the licensing consequence?
questions.explanation: Under §73-35-10(2)(b), felonies involving fraud, dishonesty, breach of trust, or money laundering are a permanent bar regardless of when they occurred. The 5-year discretion only applies to non-fraud felonies.

questions.question_text: An applicant has a felony conviction from 3 years ago for a non-fraud offense. What is the licensing result?
questions.explanation: Any felony within the past 5 years is an automatic bar to licensure under §73-35-10(2)(b). MREC discretion applies only to non-fraud felonies that occurred more than 5 years ago.

questions.question_text: A felony conviction for embezzlement disqualifies a license applicant. For how long?
questions.explanation: Embezzlement involves dishonesty and breach of trust. Under §73-35-10(2)(b), felonies involving fraud, dishonesty, or breach of trust are a permanent bar — they never become eligible for MREC discretionary review.

questions.question_text: A homeowner sells her own house without using an agent. Does she need a real estate license?
questions.explanation: Mississippi law exempts property owners from licensing requirements when selling their own property. A license is required only when performing real estate activities for others for compensation.

questions.question_text: An attorney helps a client negotiate a purchase contract as part of legal representation. Does the attorney need a license?
questions.correct_answer: No, attorneys in scope are exempt
distractors: Only if the attorney earns a fee
questions.explanation: Attorneys acting within the scope of their legal practice are exempt from Mississippi real estate licensing requirements. Miss. Code §73-35-3 exempts attorneys 'in the performance of primary or incidental duties as such attorneys at law' — negotiating a contract as part of legal representation falls within this exemption.

questions.question_text: Which activity requires a Mississippi real estate license?
questions.explanation: Property management performed for others for compensation is an activity that requires a Mississippi real estate license. Other licensed activities include selling, buying, exchanging, leasing for others, auctions, and timeshare sales.

questions.question_text: A court appoints a receiver to manage and sell a foreclosed property. Does the receiver need a license?
questions.explanation: Court-appointed individuals such as receivers, trustees, and executors are exempt from Mississippi real estate licensing requirements when performing duties ordered by the court.

questions.question_text: A state employee lists government surplus property for sale as part of her job. Does she need a license?
distractors: Yes, unless the property is vacant
questions.explanation: Miss. Code §73-35-3 exempts 'public officers while performing their duties as such,' and also salaried regular employees of a property's owner acting in the regular course of business. A state employee selling state-owned surplus property as part of her official duties therefore does not need a real estate license.
